_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.shape: The yield curve graphs Treasury yields across maturities from 1 month to 30 years, with an upward slope considered normal as longer maturities usually carry higher yields due to risk and time premiums. An inverted curve, where short-term yields exceed long-term yields, has historically preceded every modern US recession by 6 to 18 months. Currently, the 10-year Treasury yield stands at 4.38% for June 2026, and the 10-year minus 2-year spread (2s10s) is 0.28 percentage points, indicating a positively sloped curve. This contrasts with the deepest inversion in the series of -0.2 points experienced during 2022-2024 when the curve was inverted for an extended period.
notes.signal: The 2s10s spread is a widely watched recession indicator; its inversion below zero has preceded past recessions. The fact that the spread spent 2022-2024 inverted and has since re-steepened above zero indicates the curve has un-inverted, which often occurs prior to the onset of recession according to historical patterns. The NY Fed recession probability is currently 31%, reflecting moderate recession risk. Taken together, these signals suggest caution but recognize the complexity and timing uncertainty inherent in yield curve signals, especially given the observed un-inversion.
notes.now: Presently, the yield curve's positive slope with a 0.28 point spread and a 4.38% 10-year yield imply a normalization from the prior inversion period, signifying less immediate stress in the Treasury market than during 2022-2024. The 31% recession probability and recent un-inversion of the curve highlight ongoing recession risk without providing precise timing forecasts.
